title: God's Surprising Majesty: Psalm 8
speaker: Dr. Jonathan Gibson
source_files: 04-14-24 -AM- God's Surprising Majesty - Psalm 8 - GUEST SPAKER- Pastor Jonathan Gibson.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Surprise as the Key to Psalm 8
subpoints: Dr. Jonathan Gibson, guest speaker; companion passage Hebrews 2 read alongside Psalm 8
The Hebrew title of Psalm 8 is 'Per Night' — surprise; the element of surprise is what causes David to extol God in the refrain: 'O Lord, our Lord, how majestic is your name in all the earth'
Two great surprises: (1) weak little babies defeat God's enemies (v.2); (2) puny little man rules God's world (vv.3-8)

outline.title: 1. First Surprise: Strength from the Mouths of Babes (v.2)
subpoints: 'Out of the mouth of babies and infants you have established strength' — the infants here are literally sucklings; strength going in the wrong direction: a baby's mouth is supposed to receive strength, not give it out
To 'silence' the foe and avenger is the same word used in Psalm 46 for God causing wars to cease — here the ones doing the silencing are weak little babies
NT fulfillment (Matthew 21): on Palm Sunday, children running around crying 'Hosanna to the Son of David' fulfilled verse 2 — the praise of babies and infants as the strength that silences the enemy

outline.title: 2. Second Surprise: Puny Man Rules God's World (vv.3-8)
subpoints: God's 'fingers' in v.3 — anthropomorphism showing God as artist; billions of stars and galaxies at the tips of his fingers, placed like a teacher placing a gold star on a child's homework
'What is man that you are mindful of him?' — not a question of doubt but of faith; David is dumbfounded that it is true; man is a speck of dust on a rock revolving around one of billions of stars
The whole psalm turns on 'yet/but' in v.5: man is utterly insignificant (vv.3-4) yet supremely significant because he rules God's world (vv.5-8)
Darwin said we bear the image of our 'lowly origins'; Psalm 8 says we bear the image of high and holy origins — falling short only a little from the angels and from God himself
Muhammad Ali illustration: 'Superman don't need no seat belt' — 'Superman don't need no airplane.' Man rules the world, but only under God

outline.title: 3. The New Testament Reads Psalm 8 of Jesus
subpoints: David, Solomon, and other great OT figures nearly fit the picture but all fail — David commits adultery, Solomon commits adultery and idolatry
Jesus takes the title 'Son of Man' for himself; he ruled the sparrows, the lilies, evil spirits, sickness, and death; he calmed storms
Hebrews 2: Jesus 'made a little lower than the angels for the suffering of death' — he fulfills the humiliation side of Psalm 8 through his incarnation and crucifixion
Ephesians 1: all authority in this age and the age to come; 1 Corinthians 15: death itself will be placed under his feet
C.S. Lewis (Prince Caspian): 'It's not man's country — it's Aslan's country, but it is a country for a man to be king of'

outline.title: Application: Five Points
subpoints: (1) Be amazed at what you were made to be: under God, the world is your oyster — Abraham Kuyper: 'There is not a square inch over the whole of this created universe over which God does not say, mine' — and from Psalm 8 we could add: over which he does not say to us, 'yours'

(2) Be aware of what you have become: G.K. Chesterton, answering a newspaper question 'What is wrong with the world?': 'Dear Sir, I am. Yours sincerely, G.K. Chesterton' — we have all messed up the rule of the world; Romans 3:23, all have sinned and fallen short of the glory of God
(3) Be amazed at Jesus, the proper man: he is the one Son of Man who truly fulfills Psalm 8; every square inch of existence belongs to him

summary: A Sunday morning sermon by guest speaker Dr. Jonathan Gibson (Associate Professor of Old Testament, Westminster Theological Seminary), preached the same weekend as his Genesis 1:1 Sunday school. The companion passage Hebrews 2:1-18 is read alongside Psalm 8. The key to the psalm is surprise — the Hebrew title means 'per night,' and the element of surprise drives David's praise. Two great surprises: (1) weak babies defeat God's enemies (v.2), and (2) puny man rules God's world (vv.3-8). The 'fingers' of God in v.3 are an anthropomorphism showing God as artist — billions of galaxies at the tips of his fingers. The question 'what is man?' is not doubt but faith: David is dumbfounded that the creator of billions of stars actually cares for puny human beings. The whole psalm pivots on 'yet/but' in v.5: man is utterly insignificant yet supremely significant because God has crowned him with glory and honor as ruler of creation. Darwin's 'lowly origins' is directly contrasted with Psalm 8's 'high and holy origins.' The NT reads Psalm 8 as fulfilled by Jesus — Hebrews 2 quotes it of his incarnation and death; Ephesians 1 and 1 Corinthians 15 point to his final rule over all things including death. Palm Sunday fulfills v.2: children crying Hosanna as the 'strength from the mouths of babes.' Three applications are given: be amazed at what you were made to be; be aware of what you have become (G.K. Chesterton: 'What is wrong with the world? I am'); be amazed at Jesus the proper man.
transcript:
## Introduction: Surprise

Please have your Bibles open at Psalm 8. The companion passage Hebrews 2 was read alongside it. The sermon is on Psalm 8.

The Hebrew title of this psalm is *per night* — surprise. That is what is at the heart of this psalm. It is a psalm about the unexpected, the surprising. If we miss the surprise, we miss the whole point. Because it is the element of surprise that causes David to extol God's greatness in the refrain: *O Lord, our Lord, how majestic is your name in all the earth.*

We get a hint of the surprise in verse 1: the God who has set his glory above the heavens has also displayed his majesty in all the earth. When we hear that God's majesty is displayed in all the earth, we think of creation — Mount Rainier, Victoria Falls, the Grand Canyon, a star-studded night in the Appalachian Mountains. *Then sings my soul, my Savior God to thee — how great thou art.*

Yet here is the unexpected thing. When it comes to talking about God's majesty in all the earth, King David talks about babies and mankind. God's name is majestic in all the earth because, verse 2, weak little babies defeat God's enemies; and because, verses 3 to 8, puny little man rules God's world. Those are the two great surprises.

## 1. First Surprise: Strength from the Mouths of Babes (v.2)

*Out of the mouth of babies and infants you have established strength because of your foes, to still the enemy and the avenger.*

The word for infants here means sucklings — babies still in need of their mother's milk. And verse 2 says that from the mouths of these suckling babies God has established strength. Who would ever speak of strength coming from a weak, vulnerable baby? It is strength going in the wrong direction. Isn't a baby's mouth supposed to receive strength, not give it out? God reverses the direction.

The purpose: to silence the foe and the avenger. The word *silence* here is the same word used in Psalm 46 for God causing wars to cease. And who is doing the ceasing? Weak little babies. The great and mighty enemies of God are defeated not by armies or tanks or fighter planes — but by babies.

Some translations render *strength* here as *praise,* and if we had time we could look at Matthew 21 and see how Jesus applies this on Palm Sunday. When he rides into Jerusalem on a donkey — a king on a foal, a sign of humility — who recognizes him as king? The little boys and girls running around crying, *Hosanna to the Son of David.* Babies and infants. The strength of their praise silences the foe. I will return to that later.

## 2. Second Surprise: Puny Man Rules God's World (vv.3-8)

Verse 3 is comparatively longer than the other verses. It draws things out, underlining the sense of awe:

*When I look at your heavens, the work of your fingers, the moon and the stars which you have set in place.*

The mention of God's *fingers* is perhaps the most interesting thing in the verse. Does God have fingers? No — God is a spirit and does not have a body. This is what we call an anthropomorphism: speaking of God in human terms. And it is showing us that he is an artist, a painter. The metaphor conveys the delicate, intricate, detailed care with which he made the world.

But the metaphor does something else. Think of rising above the clouds in an airplane and looking out at that endless blue sky. Or think of a clear night on the mountain, away from city lights, looking up at the stars. The astronomers tell us that on a clear night you can see about 2,500 stars with the naked eye — but in reality there are hundreds of billions of stars in our galaxy and billions of galaxies. Now picture all of that at the tips of God's fingers. As if God picked up the moon like a marble and rolled it between his thumb and first finger and placed it in the night sky. As if he picked up a star and stuck it on the canvas of the night sky like a teacher peeling off a gold star and pressing it onto a child's homework.

God made the heavens with his fingers. He is a very big artist, because it is all just at the tips of his fingers.

Now if you are like me, you would expect the refrain to kick in here. But that is not how the psalm progresses. David does not exclaim praise. He asks a question:

*What is man that you are mindful of him, and the son of man that you care for him?*

The word *man* and the phrase *son of man* both speak of our fragility, our weakness, our humanness. We are minuscule specks of dust on a rock revolving around one of billions of stars in one of billions of galaxies. Why would God even bother with us?

David's question is not a question of doubt but of faith. He is not doubting that God is mindful of him. He is dumbfounded that it is true. And it is true. God cares for puny little you.

But still the refrain is withheld — because the catalyst for praise in this psalm is not the wonder of creation in verse 3, and not even God's care for man in verse 4. No. It is found in the contrast between verses 3-4 and verses 5-8. Did you notice the word *yet* — or *but* — at the beginning of verse 5? The whole psalm turns on that one word:

*Yet you have made him a little lower than the heavenly beings, and crowned him with glory and honor. You have given him dominion over the works of your hands; you have put all things under his feet.*

It is only when we see both paradoxical truths — man's insignificance and man's significance — that we burst forth in praise: *O Lord, our Lord, how majestic is your name in all the earth.*

Charles Darwin said that as human beings we bear the image of our lowly origins. Look again at verse 5: *You have made him a little lower than the heavenly beings.* We do not bear the image of lowly origins. We bear the image of high and holy origins. We fall short only a little from the angels. We fall short only a little from God himself.

And it increases. God did not only make us into a holy and high state — he also gave us a holy and high job: ruler of the world. David lists all things we rule: the realms of water, sky, and land; the fish, the birds, the animals. He is recalling Genesis 1 and saying: man is the pinnacle and prince of it all. Not just our pet hamster — the tiger and the lion. Not just the goldfish — the orcas that swim in Tacoma Bay. Not just the budgie at home — the bald eagle.

In fact, the phrase *works of your hands* in verse 6 echoes *works of your fingers* in verse 3. The very thing in verse 3 that made us feel so puny — the stars — is the very thing in verse 6 that we are given dominion over. Haven't we been to the moon and stuck a flag in it? *One small step for man, one giant leap for mankind.*

Muhammad Ali was on an airplane once. The pilot announced that all passengers should fasten their seat belts. A flight attendant noticed Ali had not put his on. He said: *Superman don't need no seat belt.* She said: *Superman don't need no airplane. Now put your seat belt on.*

That is the danger: thinking we rule the world independently from God. God is the subject of every verb in verses 5-6: *you have made him a little lower; you have crowned him; you have given him dominion; you have put all things under his feet.* Man rules the world, yes — but under God. C.S. Lewis in *Prince Caspian* has the Badger say of Narnia: *It's not man's country — it's Aslan's country — but it is a country for a man to be king of.* This world is God's world. But here is the big surprise: he made you to rule it.

## 3. Jesus: The Proper Son of Man

David and Solomon come close to fulfilling this psalm but both fail spectacularly. If none of the great men of the Old Testament fits Psalm 8, who does?

Only one man: Jesus Christ, great David's greater Son, the Prince of Peace. He took the title *Son of Man* for himself. He observed sparrows and commented on lilies. He ruled over evil spirits, sickness, and death. He calmed storms. And after his resurrection, God gave him all authority in heaven and on earth — Ephesians 1 says not only in this age but also in the age to come. First Corinthians 15 says that in the future, death itself will be placed under his feet.

Jesus: the one in whom Abraham Kuyper's quote finds its fullest meaning — *There is not a square inch in the whole of our existence over which Jesus Christ does not cry, Mine.*

And yet his beginnings were humble and insignificant. Born in Bethlehem. Fleeing to Egypt. Growing up in despised Nazareth. A carpenter's son. No former beauty that we should look upon him. During his ministry he had nowhere to lay his head. And in the end — a death befitting a criminal. Despised and rejected, a man of sorrows.

But who esteemed him? Babies and infants. When he rode into Jerusalem on a donkey — a king not on a warhorse but on the foal of a donkey — the little boys and girls running around crying *Hosanna to the Son of David* fulfilled verse 2. The praise of infants: strength to silence the foe.

## Applications

**First: Be amazed at what you were made to be.** Under God, the world is your oyster. Abraham Kuyper: *There is not a square inch over the whole of this created universe over which God does not say, Mine* — and from Psalm 8 we could add: over which he does not say to us, *Yours. It is all yours.* Puny little man does rule God's world. We photograph stars, send robots to Mars, master language, build bridges, swim with orcas, develop drugs that defeat diseases. *O Lord, our Lord, how majestic is your name in all the earth.*

**Second: Be aware of what you have become.** Yes — we have sent probes into space. But in the words of Larry Norman: *you starved your children to do it.* Yes — we've mastered language, but we twist words to tell lies. Yes — we've blown holes in mountains, but also in buildings to kill people. Yes — we've created the internet, but also used it for exploitation. G.K. Chesterton, answering a newspaper column that asked 'What is wrong with the world? Please send in your answers,' wrote: *Dear Sir, In regard to your question, what is wrong with the world? I am. Yours sincerely, G.K. Chesterton.* Man left to himself, in rebellion against his Maker, is never a pretty picture. *For all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God* (Romans 3:23).

**Third: Be amazed at Jesus, the proper man.** He is the one Son of Man who truly fulfills Psalm 8 — the one who rules the physical world, the spiritual world, the present world, the future world. Every square inch of existence is his. And because he is crowned with glory and honor, and because by his death he dealt with what we have become, he is our only hope.
